coredata.dc:description: Treatment of diabetic wounds is a major clinical issue. Diabetic wound dressings have higher requirements for anti-oxidant, antibacterial and wound monitoring properties compared to conventional wound dressings. In this study, a novel tannic acid (TA)/quaternized carboxymethyl chitosan (QCMCS)/oxidized sodium alginate (OSA)@carbon quantum dots (CQD) (TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD) hydrogels for promoting diabetic wound healing and real-time monitoring have been developed. The TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibited excellent self-healing, antibacterial and antioxidant properties. Besides, these hydrogels possessed good biocompatibility and effective hemostasis in a mouse liver injury model and significantly facilitated the healing process in a diabetic wound model. In addition, these hydrogels can reliable and timely measure the diabetic wound pH information by collecting image signals of hydrogels to monitor the healing status. Therefore, the pH responsive TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels could be utilized as wound dressing for promoting diabetic wound healing and real-time monitoring.

head.ce:title: A pH responsive tannic acid/quaternized carboxymethyl chitosan/oxidized sodium alginate hydrogels for accelerated diabetic wound healing and real-time monitoring

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Treatment of diabetic wounds is a major clinical issue. Diabetic wound dressings have higher requirements for anti-oxidant, antibacterial and wound monitoring properties compared to conventional wound dressings. In this study, a novel tannic acid (TA)/quaternized carboxymethyl chitosan (QCMCS)/oxidized sodium alginate (OSA)@carbon quantum dots (CQD) (TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD) hydrogels for promoting diabetic wound healing and real-time monitoring have been developed. The TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibited excellent self-healing, antibacterial and antioxidant properties. Besides, these hydrogels possessed good biocompatibility and effective hemostasis in a mouse liver injury model and significantly facilitated the healing process in a diabetic wound model. In addition, these hydrogels can reliable and timely measure the diabetic wound pH information by collecting image signals of hydrogels to monitor the healing status. Therefore, the pH responsive TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels could be utilized as wound dressing for promoting diabetic wound healing and real-time monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetic wounds can not only cause additional long-term treatment costs but also lead to amputation or even death if treatment failed. Diabetic wounds are difficult to heal owing to high reactive oxygen species microenvironment, recurrent infections and easy damage to the structure shape of wound dressing [1]. And, early identification of infected or non-healing wounds can help adjust treatment timely. Routine treatment of diabetic wound includes debridement, wound dressings, and antibiotics. However, single treatment is not effective due to the complex microenvironment at the diabetic wound [2]. Hydrogels are one of the most attractive candidates due to their high biocompatibility and similarity to the composition of natural extracellular matrix [3]. Therefore, there is a clinical need to develop hydrogels dressings with multiple therapeutic properties (antibacterial, antioxidant and pro-angiogenesis) to treat diabetic wounds and monitor wound healing status.

As a natural substance, quaternized carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) is currently the most widely studied natural antibacterial agent [4]. In addition, CMCS has good antibacterial activity, biodegradability, and the ability of cells to grow inward. However, the antibacterial effect of CMCS is not obvious in a neutral environment, so it is necessary to modify or functionalize CMCS [5]. Quaternized carboxymethyl chitosan (QCMCS) is a carboxymethyl chitosan derivative with a positive charge on the side quaternary ammonium group. As a cationic polymer with excellent solubility, biocompatibility, and bacteriostasis properties, QCMCS has been widely used in the biomedical field.

Marine polysaccharides show promising applications as biomedical materials due to their similar structure and function to extracellular matrix (ECM) [6]. Sodium alginate (SA) is an anionic Marine linear polysaccharide. Part of its composition can form an aldehyde group through ring-opening reaction in oxidizing environment to become oxidized sodium alginate (OSA). OSA molecular chain contains a large number of hemialdehyde groups, which can react with the amino group in QCMCS to form dynamic Schiff-base hydrogels. However, hydrogels based on a single reversible dynamic bond have poor resilience and limited fatigue resistance [5]. Tannic acid (TA) is a naturally occurring, water-soluble plant polyphenol composed of several catechol groups. The presence of phenol hydroxyl groups gives TA strong stability and antibacterial properties [7]. Additionally, TA is a potent antioxidant with the ability to effectively neutralize free radicals, thereby promoting the formation of blood vessels by reducing oxidative damage at the wound site [8]. Moreover, TA can create a favorable environment for angiogenesis by regulating the behavior of macrophages, blocking inflammatory pathways, and reducing inflammation at the wound site [9]. Therefore, the TA modification of QCMCS/OSA hydrogels can form a double network structure with QCMCS/OSA while removing reactive oxygen species, improving the mechanical properties of the hydrogels dressings and preserving the bioactive properties of each raw material to the greatest extent [10]. However, the study about TA modified QCMCS/OSA multifunctional hydrogels for wound healing has not been reported.

Early identification of the wound healing process in diabetes can help patients adjust their treatment in a timely manner. It has been reported that the wound healing process shows a good correlation with the pH of the local environment [11]. Healthy skin tissue has a pH of 4–6, while damaged skin tissue has a pH of 7–8. Therefore, establishing an accurate relationship between pH and wound healing process is important for developing effective diagnostic methods to monitor the wound healing process. With the development of nanotechnology, carbon quantum dots (CQD), as a new type of carbon nanomaterials, has attracted great interest. Due to its excellent biocompatibility, stable photoluminescence, and excellent pH response, CQD has been widely used in the fields of bioimaging [12], drug delivery [13], and novel nanosensing [14]. Recently, Zhang et al. developed a bicolorimetric polyacrylamide-quaternary ammonium chitosan‑carbon quantum dot-phenol red hydrogels for smart real-time monitoring and promotion of wound healing [11]. Chen et al. constructed a polyvinyl alcohol/iodine/sodium carboxyl cellulose/carbon quantum hydrogels to treat recurrent infections in chronic wounds [15]. However, the conjugation of CQD into these hydrogels has not been emphasized and studied. Moreover, the continuous, uncontrolled release of CQD reduces the accuracy of real-time monitoring of wound pH. Therefore, the study about conjugation of CQD into hydrogels could be necessary for avoiding the leakage of CQD from hydrogels and achieving accurate monitoring of wound pH.

In this study, a novel tannic acid/quaternized carboxymethyl chitosan/oxidized sodium alginate@carbon quantum dots (TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD) hydrogels with diabetic wound healing and realing-time monitoring had been developed (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, dynamic Schiff base QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were prepared sucessfully because the aldehyde group in the OSA reacts with the amino group on the QCMCS and CQD surfaces to form Schiff base bonds. Secondly, tannic acid (TA) was modified into the QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels to prepare the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels, in which the hydroxyl groups of the TA can react with amino groups in QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels to form hydrogen bonds. Furthermore, the physical and chemical properties and biocompatibility of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were systematically investigated. Finally, the effects of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels on the wound healing and real-time monitoring of a full-layer skin defect model in diabetic mice were evaluated.

Chitosan (CS, degree of deacetylation: 95 %, M w: 2 × 105 Da), carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS, degree of carboxylation 90 %), tannic acid (TA), ammonia, glycidyl-trimethylammonium chloride (GTMAC) and NaIO4 were provided by Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd., (Shanghai, China). Sodium alginate (SA, M w: 19.3 × 103 Da, viscosity: 200 ± 20 mPa·s, mannuronate/guluronate molar ratio (M/G ratio): 0.8), sodium citrate and ethylene glycol were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Corporation (Shanghai, China).

QCMCS was synthesized according to the relevant literature [16]. Briefly, CMCS (3.5 g) was dissolved in 150 mL of distilled water and GTMAC (0.8 g) was added. The mixture reacts at 55 °C for 48 h, using dialyzed with a dialysis bag (14 kDa) for 3 days and was frequently replaced with ultra-pure water to remove impurities. Finally, QCMCS were obtained by freeze drying.

OSA is synthesized according to the relevant literature [17]. Briefly, SA (10 g) was dissolved in 50 mL ethanol, NaIO4 (3.21 g) dissolved in SA solution, stirred at 37 °C in the dark for 12 h and 10 mL glycol was added for 60 min. Dialysis with a dialysis bag (3.5 kDa) for 3 days and often replace ultra-pure water to remove impurities. Finally, OSA were obtained by freeze drying. The concentration of aldehyde groups on OSA was determined by hydroxylamine hydrochloride potentiometric titration. Specifically: 1 g of OSA was dissolved in 250 mL solution of hydroxylamine hydrochloride with methyl orange (concentration: 0.25 mol/L) until complete dissolution. The released HCl in the solution was determined by a potentiometric titration method, with a 0.1 mol/L standard NaOH aqueous solution as the titration agent. The concentration of aldehyde groups was calculated as follows [18]:
(1)
The degree of oxidation =
V × 0.001 ×
C NaOH
× 198.11
2 × W
× 100 %
where V was the volume of NaOH consumed by titration, CNaOH was the concentration of NaOH, W was the mass of OSA.
The degree of oxidation was about 45 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino carbon quantum dots (CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g sodium citrate was dissolved in 20 mL distilled water, added to the reactor and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. Collect the supernatant, add 1200 μL ammonia, and add to the reactor, and heat it at 200 °C for 5 h. Finally, the supernatant was collected and CQD were obtained by freeze drying.

QCMCS (0.18 g), OSA (0.12 g) and CQD (1 mg) were dissolved in 2 mL phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and reacted at 37 °C for 5 min to obtain QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. The TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was obtained by soaking the QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels in a 2.5 % TA solution for 10 min.

The chemical component of QCMCS and OSA was confirmed by total reflection fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nexus). QCMCS and OSA were characterized by nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR, INOVA 600). CMCS, QCMCS, SA and OSA was dissolved in D2O. The QCMCS of degree of amino substitution (DS) were calculated at δ = 1.98 ppm (NMR maps were labeled as g) and δ = 3.13 ppm (NMR maps were labeled as 4). The DS was calculated according to the following formula [11]:
(2)
DS
QCMCS
=
I 4
/ 9 × DD %
I g
/ 3
× 100 %
where I4 and Ig were the integral regions of the proton 4 and g in the 1H NMR spectra, respectively; DD% was the degree of deacetylation of CS.

The microstructure of CQD were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). Briefly, CQD were dripped onto a copper mesh and observed. The crystal structure of CQD was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Empyrean). The chemical elements and bonding structures of CQD were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi). The absorption spectrum of CQD was determined by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Lambda 750 S).

The content of TA was tested by the deduction method with UV–vis [19]. The QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was dried to constant weight (W1). The amount of TA was obtained by subtracting the amount of TA in the solution after soaking the QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels (W3) from the initial amount of TA in the solution (W2). The concentration was obtained from the standard curve equation of TA. The relative TA loading in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogel was calculated as follows [20]:
(3)
Content of
TA =
W 2
−
W 3
W 1
× 100 %

The micromorphology of hydrogels was studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-IT200). Before SEM, the hydrogels were freeze by liquid nitrogen and freeze-dried at −50 °C for 24 h. The chemical composition of hydrogels was determined by total reflection fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nexus). The fluorescence properties of the hydrogels were detected with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (970 CRT). Furthermore, the hydrogels were prepared into cylindrical (15 mm high, 20 mm diameter) and compression testing were measured at 37 °C and 40 % humidity using CMT4503-5KN microcomputer control electronic universal testing machine (Jinan, China). Oscillatory frequency scanning (from 0.1 to 10 Hz), strain amplitude scanning (from 0.1 % to 1000 %), and alternate step strain scanning (1 % and 200 %) were performed using a rotating rheometer with flat geometry (Kinexus Pro+) at 37 °C. The energy storage modulus, loss modulus and self-healing behavior of hydrogels were evaluated.

The original weight of the hydrogels was recorded as Wd, and then the hydrogels were immersed in 100 mL PBS at 37 °C. After reaching equilibrium, the hydrogels were taken out and the excess PBS was removed with a filter paper and the weight of the swollen hydrogels was recorded as Ww. All experiments were repeated three times. The expansion rate was calculated as follows [3]:
(4)

The antioxidant capacity of hydrogels was evaluated by the scavenging ability of 1, 1-diphenyl-2-pyridinium (DPPH) free radicals. DPPH-ethanol solution (8 mL, 0.04 mmol/L) was mixed with hydrogels extract (0.1 g/mL) in equal volume. After incubation for 30 min, 200 μL supernatant was collected and the optical density (OD) value of 517 nm was determined by microplate reader (MULTISKAN GO). All experiments were repeated three times. The DPPH radical gap was calculated as follows [21]:
(5)

where Ao was the OD value of the hydrogels group; Ac was the OD value of the DPPH group; As was the OD value of hydrogels mixed with DPPH group; D was the antioxidant properties of hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The release of TA and CQD
ce:section.ce:para:
The standard method was used to measure the release of TA from the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels [22]. The TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was put in a 10 mL 0.5 mg/mL NaHSO3 solution with a pH of 5 to 8. After a certain amount of time, the 200 μL supernatant was collected and added to 200 μL 3.5 mg/mL ferric ammonium citrate solution. Subsequently, it was mixed with 1 mL water and 200 μL 8 mg/mL ammonia solution. After incubation for 10 min, the absorbance at 525 nm was measured. The concentration of TA was calculated by constructing a standard curve at the same conditions. In addition, after each sampling, the same solution was added to the hydrogels solution to keep the total volume of the solution constant. The cumulative release rate of TA was calculated according to the following formula [20]:
(6)
Cumulative release rate of
TA =
V t
∑ 1
n − 1
C i
+
V o
C n
W
× 100 %
where Vt was the volume of the solution at the nth test, Cn was the concentration of the solution at the nth test, Vo was the total volume of the initial solution, and W was the total mass of TA in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels.

The concentration vs time was fitted to zero order, first order, Higuchi order and Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic models, to determine the release mechanism of TA. The kinetic models were expressed as follows [23]:
(7)
Zero order model : Q = kt +
Q o
(8)
First order model : Q =
Q o

e kt
(9)

(10)

kt n
where Q was the amount of TA release at time t, Qo was the initial TA released amount, k was the rate constant, and n was the release exponent.
The release of CQD was analyzed and fitted by the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus as a gram-positive model) and Escherichia coli (E. coli as a gram-negative model) were used to determine the antibacterial activity of the hydrogels. The recovered bacterial solution (106 CFU/mL) was co-cultured with PBS、QCMCS/OSA、QCMCS/OSA@CQD、TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels for 24 h, PBS was used as controls. The obtained bacterial/hydrogel material was divided into three parts. During the coating plate experiment, 100 μL diluted bacterial solution was coated on the agar plate and cultured for 24 h. In the other part, the hydrogel was held in place with a 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution, followed by gradient dewatering with an ethanol solution. After freeze-drying, the bacterial structure on the surface of the hydrogels were observed by SEM. In the third part, the optical density value (OD) at 600 nm were measured [3].

Hydrogels extract were used to evaluate the cell activity of the hydrogels. Mouse fibroblasts L929 (20,000 cells/well) were inoculated in 24-well plates and then cultured in a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The medium was replaced with hydrogels extract and L929 cells were cultured for 24 h, 48 h and 72 h. Finally, CCK-8 reagent was added and incubated in an incubator for 3 h. The optical density value (OD) was measured at 450 nm. In addition, cells were stained with PI (red fluorescence for labeled dead cells) and Calcein AM (green fluorescence for labeled living cells) for 30 min after 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h of culture and observed.

C57BL/6 mice (8-week female) were anesthetized by intrabitoneal injection of 4 % pentobarbital sodium (0.1 mL/10.0 g body weight). The abdominal incision exposed the liver, a filter paper was placed under the liver, a 23G needle was used to puncture the liver, and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were quickly applied to the blood exit. The livers of the control mice did not undergo any treatment after the puncture. After three minutes, the weight of the filter paper containing the blood was measured.

The blood compatibility of QCMCS/OSA, QCMCS/OSA@CQD, TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were evaluated using rat red blood cells. In short, 200 μL red blood cell suspension was mixed with 800 μL hydrogels extract, and distilled water and PBS were selected as positive and negative controls. After incubation for 1 h and centrifugation, 200 μL supernatant was taken and measured at 545 nm for optical density (OD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing in vivo of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para:
Construction of a mouse model of diabetes: a reference to our previous work. Next, 25 diabetic female mice were randomly divided into five groups. After anesthesia with 4 % pentobarbital sodium (0.1 mL/10 g body weight), after removal of hair on the back, disinfection with 0.5 % iodophor, and then surgical scissors were used to cut a wound with a diameter of about 1 cm on the back of the diabetic mice. The diabetic mice were then treated with QCMCS/OSA, QCMCS/OSA@CQD, TA/QCMCS/OSA, and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. Control diabetic mice were treated with commercial dressing (Tegaderm™ Film). Wounds of the diabetic mice were photographed at days 0, 3, 6, 9, and 12 days, and wound pH were recorded by precision pH strips and fluorescence photographs (fired at 365 nm). The wound healing rate (WHR) was calculated as follows [24]:
(11)
WHR =
W o
−
W t
W o
× 100 %
where Wo was the initial area of the wound; Wt was the area of the wound at time.
After 12 days, all diabetic mice were euthanized. Wound tissue was then collected, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, embedded in paraffin, sliced, and stained with H&E, Masson, and CD31.
All animal procedures followed the National Research Council's Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, and approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Wuhan University of Technology.

Each experiment was repeated 5 times. SPSS (Statistical Product and Service Solutions) software was used for statistical analysis, and all experimental data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. (***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01, *p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CQD was observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (a-b), CQD was a quasi-spherical particle with an average diameter of 3.9 ± 0.8 nm. In addition, in order to explore the structure and functional groups of CQD, the crystallinity and surface functional groups of CQD were researched by XRD and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1(c), CQD has a wide peak at 23.8°, which indicated that the prepared CQD belongs to the amorphous carbon structure [25]. At the same time, three distinct XPS peaks were detected at 284.8, 530.4, and 397.6 eV (Fig. S1) corresponding to the C, O, and N elements, respectively. It was worth noting that the C 1S spectrum showed that the C element in CQD mainly existed in the form of CC, CO and CN [26]. The O 1S spectra showed that the O elements in CQD mainly existed in the form of CO and C-OH [26]. The N 1S spectrum showed that the N element in CQD existed mainly in the form of NH, CN, and CN [27]. The XPS results indicated the presence of -NH2 and -OH groups on the surface of the CQD prepared in this study. These groups made the CQD good water-soluble and biocompatible. At the same time, the -NH2 on the surface of the CQD could also form a Schiff base bond with the aldehyde group of the OSA and fix the CQD in the TA/QCMCS/OSA hydrogels, thus improving the stability of the CQD in the hydrogels.

QCMCS was synthesized by grafting CMCS with GTMAC to obtain good solubility and antibacterial properties. The QCMCS were then characterized by 1H NMR. As shown in Fig. S2, QCMCS had a proton peak at 3.1 ppm attributed to the trimethyl quaternary ammonium proton on GTMAC. The proton peaks at 2.5, 4.1 and 3.2 ppm corresponded to the quaternary ammonium group. In addition, the remaining proton peaks in the 1H NMR spectra were attributed to proton signals on the carboxymethyl chitosan backbone, indicating that GTMAC was successfully grafted onto carboxymethyl chitosan [28]. According to 1H NMR calculations, the degree of substitution (DS) of GTMAC was 27.8 %.

The molecular structure and functional group of OSA can be confirmed by FTIR and 1H NMR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1(d), both SA and OSA exhibited wide hydroxyl stretching vibration absorption peaks in the range of 4000–3000 cm−1 [29]. It was worth noting that the SA showed the main characteristic peak at 2933 cm−1 due to the CH stretching vibrations of the polysaccharide structure and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the -COO- [30]. In contrast to SA, OSA showed a new characteristic peak at 1726 cm−1 attributed to the CO absorption vibrational peak of the aldehyde group [31]. These results indicated that the hydroxyl groups adjacent to C-2 and C-3 on the alginate-glucuronide monomer were oxidized to the aldehyde group by NaIO4 [32]. In addition, the OSA and SA exhibited similar FTIR spectroscopy curves, suggesting that the periodate ion cleaves the C2-C3 bond by oxidation alone to form the dihydrate. Fig. S3 showed the 1H NMR spectra of the SA and OSA. In contrast to the SA, a new proton peak attributed to the formation of a hemiacetal proton by aldehydes with adjacent hydroxyl groups was found at 5.6 ppm in the OSA spectrum [33]. These results above suggested the OSA was prepared succesfully.

The FTIR spectra of QCMCS, OSA, QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was shown in Fig. 1(e). For QCMCS, the absorption peak at 1413 cm−1 can be observed, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of the ammonia (−NH2) group in QCMCS [34]. In the spectrum of QCMCS/OSA@CQD, the vibrational frequency at 1413 cm−1 is slightly blue-shifted due to the introduction of OSA and CQD. Moreover, the absorption and vibrational peaks at 1726 cm−1 disappear, while those at 1406 cm−1 are weakened because the aldehyde group in the OSA reacts with the amino group on the QCMCS and CQD surfaces to form Schiff base bonds. As shown in Fig. 1(f), TA exhibited CO tensile vibration peak (1720 cm−1), CO tensile vibration peak (1615 cm−1), C=C-C aromatic ring peak (1444 cm−1) and CO peak (1193 cm−1) [35]. The characteristic peaks of QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were found in the FTIR curve of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. The absence of the vibrational peak of TA at 1720 cm−1 may be caused by the formation of amide bonds between the carboxyl group of TA and the amino group of the hydrogels. In addition, the vibrational peak at 1406 cm−1 is redshifted to 1412 cm−1 and the intensity of the absorption peak is significantly reduced, possibly due to the formation of hydrogen bonds [36]. Therefore, the results of FTIR analysis indicated the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were prepared succcessfully.

The surface morphologies of hydrogels were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the SEM images of the QCMCS/OSA hydrogels showed typical cross-linked porous network structure with an average pore size of 124.8 ± 10.9 μm (Fig. 2(b)) and a porosity of 77.5 ± 1.1 % (Fig. 2(c)) owing to Schiff base reactions between amino groups in QCMCS and aldehyde groups in OSA. Similarly, the QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels also had cross-linked porous network structures with an average pore size of 130.1 ± 10.2 μm and a porosity of 76.8 ± 0.8 %. It indicated that the addition of CQD had no obvious effect on the pore structure of the QCMCS/OSA hydrogels. However, TA modification made the network structure of the hydrogels more compact and denser. TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels possessed a cross-linked network structures with smaller average pore size (76.8 ± 12.6 and 79.2 ± 12.9 μm, respectively) and porosity of (68.3 ± 0.9 % and 67.4 ± 0.8 %, respectively). On the one hand, the hydroxyl group of the TA was connected to amino groups in QCMCS/OSA to form hydrogen bonds; on the other hand, the hydroxyl group in TA could undergo aldol condensation reactions with the residual aldehyde groups in QCMCS/OSA. The highly interconnected porous network structure of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels promotes the diffusion of oxygen and molecules and provides the right conditions for wound healing.

The good swelling properties of the hydrogels can absorb excess exudate from the wound, reduce secondary infections and accelerate wound healing [3]. The volume swelling ratio of different hydrogels was quantitatively determined in PBS solution at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 2(d), all hydrogels reached the equilibrium swelling state after 12 h. The equilibrium swelling ratio of QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogel was 310.6 % and 301.2 %, respectively. In contrast, the equilibrium swelling ratios of TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were reduced to 250.3 % and 240.6 %, respectively. Obviously, the modification of TA increased the cross-linkage density of the hydrogels and suppressed the diffusion of water molecules, leading to a decrease in the equilibrium swelling ratio of the hydrogels [37]. The remarkable swelling properties of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels can facilitate the diffusion of liquids into the hydrogels structure and effectively absorb exudates.

In diabetic wounds, excessive and uncontrolled ROS leads to persistent oxidative stress that severely delays wound repair [38]. DPPH was used to quantitatively determine the radical scavenging rate of hydrogels [7]. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the DPPH clearance rates of QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD were 6.4 ± 1.5 % and 6.8 ± 1.7 %, respectively. After TA modification, the DPPH clearance rates of hydrogels was increased obviously. And the DPPH clearance rates of TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD were 88.3 ± 2.3 % and 87.9 ± 1.8 %, respectively. The excellent capacity of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was attributed to the phenolic hydroxyl group in TA can effectively inhibit oxidation reactions by losing hydrogen atoms on the hydroxyl group, forming radicals and neutralizing other radicals. These results indicated that the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibited high antioxidant capacity and could reduce ROS levels in diabetic wounds to protects wounds from oxidative damage, and promote diabetic wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-healing and mechanical properties of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Suitable self-healing and mechanical properties can maintain the structural shape and the protective function of the hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 3(a), a complete TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels is cut into two parts, one of which was stained with phenol red. Then the two hydrogels with different colors were placed together. Without any external intervention, the two hydrogels with different colors were able to reassemble into a new hydrogel within 30 min at room temperature. These results indicated that the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels possessed excellent self-healing property. The self-healing ability of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was quantitatively evaluated by step oscillation (Fig. 3(b)). The hydrogels maintain its gel state at 1 % strain, while the gel structure was destroyed at 200 % strain. When high strain was removed, the hydrogels gradually recovers its integrity and G' returns to its initial value due to the self-healing of the Schiff base bond and hydrogen bonds in the hydrogels, showing good self-healing.

Wound dressings are often subjected to external stresses caused by wound motion. Hydrogels wound dressings with excellent mechanical properties are able to withstand pressure and extend their life. As shown in Fig. 3(c-d), due to the cross-linked network formed by Schiff base reaction, the fracture stresses of QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were 62.6 ± 2.3 kPa and 56.3 ± 3.1 kPa, respectively. After TA modification, the fracture stresses of TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels reached 95.1 ± 2.6 kPa and 88.6 ± 3.2 kPa, respectively. The good compressive property of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was attributed to the formation of a double-crosslinked network structure between TA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD, which significantly improves the mechanical properties of the hydrogels. On the one hand, the aldehyde group in the OSA reacts with the amino group on the QCMCS and CQD surfaces to form Schiff base bonds. Secondly, the hydroxyl group of the TA can react with amino groups in QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels to form hydrogen bonds. In addition, under low stress, the energy storage modulus (G') of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels is higher than the loss modulus (G') (Fig. 3(e)). As the pressure increases, G' of the hydrogels starts to decrease gradually. When the strain exceeded 157 %, the G' and G“ curves intersected, indicating that the double-network structure of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels completely collapsed. In addition, the G' of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was always greater than G" in the frequency scanning range of 0.1–10 Hz (Fig. S4), indicating that the hydrogels possessed structural stability and good elasticity. These results show that TA effectively improves the mechanical properties of the QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. In a word, the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibited excellent self-healing ability and good mechanical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The release of TA from the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: First, we determined TA loading in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels, which was 1.3 %. Due to the strong supramolecular interaction between TA and polysaccharide molecules mediated by hydroxyl groups, a large amount of TA was bound in the QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogel network. As shown in Fig. S5, the cumulative release of TA gradually increased in the first 24 h and leveled off after 24 h. Moreover, the cumulative release of TA in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was positively correlated with pH. When pH = 8, the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels released 42.6 % of the total TA within 72 h. In contrast, when pH = 5, the cumulative release of TA from TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels accounted for only 13.2 % of the total TA. The lower TA release of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels at pH = 5 was attributed to the protonation of the amino groups in QCMCS, which improves the binding strength between QCMCS and TA [39]. The above results indicated that the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels can release TA in a pH-responsive manner, which was beneficial for regulating the microenvironment of diabetic wounds.

To evaluate kinetic of TA release, the profiles of TA release presented in Fig. S5 were assessed by various mathematical models (e.g. zero order, first order, Higuchi order, and Korsmeyer-Peppas model). The kinetic model of different pH value was considered by determination of highest degree of correlation coefficient (R2) as shown in Table S1. When pH was 5 to 8, the release behavior of TA followed the Korsmeyer-Peppas model. It was possible that crosslinking density of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was insufficient to control TA release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound pH monitoring properties of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The pH in the wound environment plays a key role in the wound healing process. Normal skin has a pH of 5–6 and is acidic. When skin was injured, the pH of the wound rises to between 7 and 8 due to microvessel leakage, which delays inflammation and delays wound healing [11]. Based on the stable photoluminescence properties and good pH response of CQD, a novel TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels were developed to construct a pH-dependent monitoring system with regular fluorescence intensity variations by conjugation of CQD with OSA. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the color of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels changes from blue to black as the pH increases. By this change of color, the healing of the wound can be visually observed. In addition, we further tested the fluorescence variation of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels at different pH of PBS solution. As shown in Fig. 4(b-c), at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels gradually decreases with the increase of pH value. By establishing the normalization curves, we find that the fluorescence intensity and pH of the hydrogels show a good linear relationship, with a 98 % fit. This linear correlation of fluorescence intensity with pH was useful for real-time monitoring of wound status in clinical settings.

In addition, we explored the in vitro cumulative release of CQD from TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels in different pH at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of CQD at different concentrations in PBS solution was positively correlated with the normalization curve. The TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was immersed in different pH and the fluorescence intensity of the different pH solution was measured at different time points. The cumulative release of CQD in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels within 72 h was inversely proportional to different pH value. The TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibited the highest CQD release at pH = 5.5, which may be attributed to the dominant role of Schiff base fracture [40]. Additionally, the maximum cumulative release of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels at different pH was 5.5 μg (Fig. 4(d)), which did not exceed 1 % of the total CQD (1000 μg) in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. Table S2 showed the various mathematical models of CQD release at different pH value. When pH was 5 to 8, CQD showed good fit for Korsmeyer-Peppas model suggesting the crosslinking density of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels could cause solvents to permeate the hydrogels. Additionally, it also followed zero order and Higuchi order. It could be due to the action of Schiff base bond between the amino groups of CQD and the aldehyde groups of OSA resulting in obstruction of CQD release. Therefore, the CQD was not easily dissociated from the hydrogels, ensuring the fluorescence stability of hydrogels in wound monitoring.

It is reported that the three primary colors of red, green, and blue (RGB) can be combined to create almost any color that can be seen by the naked eye. Current systems for wound pH monitoring include fiber-optic signal measurements and other acid-base indicators [41]. However, fiber signals were susceptible to contamination, and acid-base indicators have certain cytotoxic properties that may yield inaccurate wound pH signals. In this study, we use an image processor to convert the fluorescence colors of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels into RGB signals via a corresponding linear equation, which enables the intelligent acquisition of accurate pH for wounds. As shown in Fig. 4(e), under the excitation light of 365 nm, when pH increases, the values of R, G and B gradually decrease, and the signal was quantified by calculating the arithmetic average of R, G and B. The specific calculation formula was as follows:
(12)
pH =
1032.9 −
R + G + B
111.6

The high glycemic environment of diabetic wounds was conducive to bacterial reproduction, which delays wound healing. The good antibacterial activity was important for hydrogels dressing [42]. In this study, E. coli and S. aureus were used to assess the antibacterial activity of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. The bacteriostatic properties of these sample in physiological environments were evaluated by the coated plate method. As shown in Fig. 5 (a-c), the survival rate of S. aureus in the control groups (PBS), QCMCS/OSA, QCMCS/OSA@CQD, TA, TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups were 100 %, 48 %, 46 %, 57 %, <1 % and < 1 %, respectively. Besides, the survival rates of E. coli in the control groups (PBS), QCMCS/OSA, QCMCS/OSA@CQD, TA, TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups were 100 %, 41 %, 42 %, 49 %, <1 % and < 1 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. S7, the results of optical density test were consistent with that of the plate counting method. Obviously, the QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibited antibacterial activity owing to the inherent hemostatic and antibacterial activities of QCMCS [5]. Interestingly, after TA modification of hydrogels, the number of colonies and OD values of both bacteria in the TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups decrease significantly. In addition, SEM was used to observe the morphology of bacteria treated with different samble. As shown in Fig. 5(d), after co-culture for 24 h, the bacterial cell membranes in the control groups were smooth and flat. However, the bacterial cell membranes in the TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups were exhibited higher damage than that in the TA、QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups. Therefore, these results indicated the TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels have better antibacterial properties than QCMCS/OSA, TA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. Moreover, TA modification improved antibacterial activity of QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. On the one hand, quaternized chitosan and its derivatives have been reported to have antibacterial effects by electrostatically destroying bacterial cell walls [43]. On the other hand, TA can destroy the integrity of bacterial cell membranes by binding to bacterial cell walls via hydrogen bonding [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytocompatibility and hemostatic properties of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: A versatile hydrogel dressing should have good biocompatibility properties. In this study, the in vitro hemocompatibility of the QCMCS/OSA, QCMCS/OSA@CQD, TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was studied using red blood cells. The results of in vitro hemocompatibility of the samples were shown in Fig. S8. Obviously, the supernatant treated with PBS and hydrogels was transparent, while the supernatant in the deionized water group was red, indicating the rupture of red blood cells. The hemolysis rate was <5 % in all these hydrogels groups, whch indicated that these hydrogels had good blood compatibility.

The biocompatibility of the QCMCS/OSA, QCMCS/OSA@CQD, TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels in vitro was evaluated by Cell Count Kit-8 (CCK-8) and Live/Dead fluorescence staining. The proliferation of L929 cells after 24, 48 and 72 h of culture on these hydrogels were analyzed using CCK-8 assay (Fig. 6(a)). The results showed that the cell viability of L929 cells is above 95 % in these hydrogels group and has no obvious change compared to that in the control group, indicating excellent biocompatibility of these hydrogels. The survival status of L929 cells at different time points were measured by staining of living/dead cells with Calcein-AM/PI. The Fluorescence staining images of L929 cells cocultured with extracts from these hydrogels for 24 h, 48 h and 72 h were shown in Fig. 6(b). Obviously, most of the cells were green and almost no red dead cells were observed. These results of Live/Dead fluorescence staining supported that of CCK8 assay. Therefore, the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibited excellent in vitro biocompatiblity.

In addition, a mouse liver hemorrhage model was used to test the hemostatic properties of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 6(c-d), compared with the blank control group, the blood loss in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels group reduced liver blood loss by about 70 %. The excellent hemostatic properties of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels may be attributed to the positively charged quaternary ammonium group in QCMCS that can promotes platelet aggregation, thus accelerating the hemostasis process [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing and monitoring capabilities of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: A full-layer skin defect model in diabetic mice was developed to evaluate the therapeutic effect of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels on wound healing. As shown in Fig. 7 (a-b), the healing effect of the TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups was significantly better than that of the control group (Tegadrem™ Film), QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. Bacterial experiments also found that TA inhibited bacterial growth, suggesting that the modification of TA on hydrogels had a beneficial effect on the healing of diabetic wounds. On Day 12, the wound skin in the TA/QCMCS/OSA and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups appeared close to normal skin, with wound healing rate of 96 % and 97 %, respectively. In contrast, diabetic wounds in the QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups were significantly unhealed, with wound healing rate of 80 % and 83 %, respectively. These results suggest that TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels significantly promotes wound healing in diabetic mice.

The TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels showed a stable pH response in vitro. In vivo pH monitoring property was further investigated. Based on the excellent pH-responsiveness of the pH-sensitive TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels in vitro, a full-layer skin defect model in diabetic mice was used to test the ability of the pH responsive TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels to monitor pH of the wound in vivo. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels covered the wounds of the diabetic mice. The color of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels changeed from Day 0 to Day 3 under the excitation of visible and 365 nm UV light. In Fig. 8(b), the test pH of the diabetic mice wound on Day 0 was 5.5. After 1 day, the pH of the wound of the diabetic mice increased significantly, reaching a peak of 7.6, which could due to severe infection caused by rapid bacterial multiplication due to the highly oxidized microenvironment of diabetic wounds [46]. After 2 days, the pH of the wounds of the diabetic mice was decreased to 6.4, as the antibacterial and antioxidant properties of the hydrogels cleared the wounds of infection and high ROS concentration. After 3 days, the pH of the wounds of the diabetic mice gradually returned to normal levels due to local fluid adjustment. More importantly, the true pH tested using precision pH strips correspond well with the pH determined by monitoring the color change of the hydrogels, which further demonstrates the good pH response of TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels. These results demonstrate that TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels exhibit accurate diagnostic capability.

HE staining is one of the effective methods to assess wound healing at the histological level [3]. Fig. 9 (a-d) shows the results of HE staining, Masson trichromatic staining and CD31 immunofluorescence staining. The Tegaderm™ film group had a high number of inflammatory cells on day 12, loose epithelial tissue and almost no skin appendages such as hair follicles and blood vessels. Because QCMCS possessed antibacterial ability and could reduce infection, the QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups have more new hair follicles than the Tegaderm™ Film group. More importantly, compared with the QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups, the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels group showed more hair follicles and thinner epithelial tissue (58.8 ± 2.2 μm) owing to the synergistic antibacterial effect between TA and QCMCS, similar to the epithelial thickness of normal skin [47].

In addition, collagen deposition is a key process in the repair of tissue structure and function during wound healing. The QCMCS/OSA and QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels groups had higher collagen deposition than the Tegaderm™ Film group, indicating that the elimination of wound infection had a favorable effect on collagen deposition. After the addition of TA, due to the removal of high concentration ROS and further sterilization, the collagen deposition in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels group was the most significant (73.2 ± 1.1 %), distributed in various tissues including epidermis and dermis. CD31 was a marker of vascular endothelial differentiation and was used to promote the formation of blood vessels during the late stages of wound healing [48]. After CD31 and DAPI staining, a large number of neovascularization were found in the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels group, with a relative expression of 512.8 ± 35.1 %. Because the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels can promote angiogenesis by modulating the expression of angiogenic growth factors through antibacterial and antioxidant effects. These results further support the effectiveness of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels in accelerating wound healing.

To evaluate the toxicity of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels in diabetic mice. The degradation of the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels was studied by subcutaneous implantation. Major organs of the hydrogels group and control group were collected 21 days after subcutaneous implantation for histological analysis. As can be seen from Fig. S9, the hydrogels implanted under the skin basically did not cause inflammation and histological lesions after degradation. These results indicated that the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels had good in vivo compatibility.

In conclusion, a novel pH responsive TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels for accelerated diabetic wound healing and real-time monitoring were developed successfully. These hydrogels exhibited a stable porous network structure, good biocompatibility and antioxidant properties, excellent self-healing properties and antibacterial properties. These properties enable the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels to provide a good repair environment for wounds and accelerate the rapid healing of diabetic wounds. Besides, the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels can obtain accurate pH indication of the diabetic wound to realize intelligent real-time monitoring of the wound healing process. Therefore, the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogels have great potential in diabetic wounds treatment and management.

All authors have agreed to the manuscript submission and confirm that the work has not been submitted simultaneously to another journal for consideration. In addition, all authors declare no conflicts of interest.
